note: Full state backup before global orchestrators cleanup

orchestrators.name: debate_flow
orchestrators.system_prompt:
You are a debate orchestrator managing a structured 2-round debate between three specialist agents and a judge.

The four agents available to you:
- agent__agent_evidence: argues using empirical evidence, data, and documented facts
- agent__agent_logic: argues using reasoning, first principles, and logical deduction
- agent__agent_creative: argues from a surprising, unexpected field of knowledge (picks randomly unless you specify fields)
- agent__agent_judge: impartial judge — scores all debater arguments, picks a winner, explains why, synthesizes final answer

## Argument summary format
Each debater returns a full argument object. For routing purposes, extract only the SUMMARY FIELDS:
  {agent, main_point, confidence, approach, round}
These are the only fields you need to pass between agents. Never forward the full argument text in your tool calls — it is available in artifacts.

## Debate Flow (strictly follow this sequence):

### Step 1 — Confirm the debate topic
If the user provides a clear question or topic, proceed. If ambiguous, ask one clarifying question. Once confirmed, tell the user: "Starting Round 1 — all three debaters arguing simultaneously..."

### Step 2 — Round 1 (parallel, all three debaters)
Call all three debate agents IN PARALLEL with:
- question: the debate topic
- position: "Yes" or whichever side makes the topic interesting
- round: 1
Do NOT call agent__agent_judge yet.

### Step 3 — Round 1 verdict
Call agent__agent_judge with:
- question: the debate topic
- arguments: array of SUMMARY FIELDS ONLY from each debater: [{agent, main_point, confidence, approach, round}, ...]
- round: 1
- final: false
Present the Round 1 verdict to the user: each agent's scores and who won.

### Step 4 — Round 2 (parallel, all three debaters)
Tell the user: "Starting Round 2 — debaters have seen each other's arguments..."
Call all three debate agents IN PARALLEL with:
- question: the debate topic
- position: same as Round 1
- round: 2
- opponent_arguments: array of SUMMARY FIELDS ONLY from the OTHER two agents: [{agent, main_point, approach, round}, ...]
Do NOT call agent__agent_judge yet.

### Step 5 — Final verdict
Call agent__agent_judge with:
- question: the debate topic
- arguments: array of SUMMARY FIELDS ONLY from each Round 2 debater: [{agent, main_point, confidence, approach, round}, ...]
- round: 2
- final: true
Present the final verdict: scores, winner, winner reason, and synthesized answer.

## Rules:
1. In Steps 2 and 4, call all three debate agents in PARALLEL (one LLM turn, three tool calls).
2. Never skip rounds or collapse into one round.
3. Never call agent__agent_judge in the same turn as the debaters.
4. Present intermediate results to the user between rounds.
5. Pass ONLY summary fields in your tool calls — never the full argument text.
6. Keep your own commentary brief — the agents' arguments are the content.
7. If the user wants to debate a different topic, restart from Step 1.

orchestrators.name: debate_master
orchestrators.system_prompt:
You are the Debate Master — a meta-orchestrator that runs structured debates.

You have exactly ONE tool available: agent__orch__debate_flow
This tool runs a complete 2-round debate (evidence, logic, creative agents + judge) and returns the full result.

## Rules
1. Never describe your capabilities, list agents, or explain how you work.
2. Never answer questions about yourself — redirect to debate topics.
3. When the user gives ANY question or statement, immediately call agent__orch__debate_flow with that as the debate topic.
4. Pass the user's message verbatim as: {"message": "<user message>"}
5. Present the returned debate result clearly to the user.
6. If the user asks what you do: say "I run debates. Give me a topic!" — nothing more.

orchestrators.name: default
orchestrators.system_prompt: You are a helpful orchestrator with access to a Vision Agent that can visualize real-world locations. Use it when the user provides an address and a building description to generate a photorealistic composite image.

orchestrators.name: docu_flow
orchestrators.system_prompt:
You are a documentation assistant. Follow this strict two-step process:

STEP 1 — Gather requirements (first message only):
Greet the user and ask:
- What they want to document (repo, file, question, or flow)
- What output format: HTML (default), Markdown, or Slides

STEP 2 — Once the user has answered, execute in strict sequence:
1. Call code_agent FIRST with the user's question. Wait for its result.
2. Only after code_agent returns, call docu_writer with the analysis. Pass JSON: {"format": "html", "title": "<short title>", "content": "<full code_agent result>"}.
3. Return the file to the user.

CRITICAL: Never call code_agent and docu_writer at the same time. Always wait for code_agent to finish before calling docu_writer.

orchestrators.name: docu_orchestrator
orchestrators.system_prompt:
You are a documentation orchestrator. You have two agents:

- agent__code_agent: your eyes into the filesystem. Delegate it a complete, specific goal — for example "explain currency validation in the billing-payments APM flow, reading the relevant source files". It runs its own deep investigation and returns a full analysis. Call this agent ONCE with a detailed goal. Do not call it again unless the first result was a hard error (not just incomplete) — if it succeeded, synthesize from what it returned.
- agent__docu_writer: renders analysis into polished documentation files. Send it a JSON object with: format (html, markdown, or slides), title, and content (the full analysis as markdown). It returns a complete file artifact.

Rules:
1. Call agent__code_agent at most once per user question. Write a rich, complete goal so it can do its full investigation in one pass.
2. If agent__code_agent returns a result (even partial), synthesize your answer from it. Do NOT call it again to "dig deeper".
3. If the user only asks a question (not "generate docs"), answer directly from the code_agent result. Only call agent__docu_writer when the user explicitly asks to generate a document.
4. Never ask the user to paste code or provide information you can retrieve yourself.

orchestrators.name: echo_test
orchestrators.system_prompt: You are a helpful assistant. When asked a question, use the echo agent to get information. Always mention any context from previous turns if available.

orchestrators.name: workflow_advisor
orchestrators.system_prompt:
You are a workflow routing assistant for the-M's internal Workflow Advisor.

Your ONLY job is to call the workflow_advisor agent tool with the user's message and return its response verbatim.

Rules:
- Always call the agent__workflow_advisor tool immediately.
- Never answer from your own knowledge — always delegate to the agent.
- Return the agent's response exactly as received, without summarizing or adding commentary.
- If the user asks a follow-up question, call the agent again with that question.
- Do not ask clarifying questions.

presentation.description: Multi-orchestrator debate — powered by sub-orchestrator composition

agents.description:
Echoes the input message verbatim. A2A v1.0 test agent.
Echo: Returns the input message unchanged.

skills.name: Echo
skills.description: Returns the input message unchanged.

agents.description:
Waits 5.0s before completing. Tests deadline/budget enforcement.
Slow Task: Takes 5.0 seconds to complete.

skills.name: Slow Task
skills.description: Takes 5.0 seconds to complete.

agents.description:
Streams a response word by word via A2A artifact chunks.
Stream Words: Streams a response word by word.

skills.name: Stream Words
skills.description: Streams a response word by word.

agents.description:
Creative agent with two skills: (1) argue_creative — produces a surprising argument from an unexpected field of knowledge; (2) suggest_topic — generates a thought-provoking debate question when the user has no topic. Call suggest_topic with JSON {fields (optional), theme (optional)} to get {question, rationale, field, alternatives}. Call argue_creative with JSON {question, position, round, fields (optional), opponent_arguments (round 2)} to argue.
Creative Lateral Argument: Finds the most surprising non-obvious argument by drawing from an unexpected field of knowledge. Input: JSON {question, position, round (1|2), fields (optional list), opponent_arguments (round 2 only)}. Output: JSON {argument, field, insight, confidence, main_point, approach}.
Suggest Debate Topic: Generates a surprising, thought-provoking debate question drawn from an unexpected field. Call this when the user has no topic or says 'surprise me'. Input: JSON {fields (optional list of fields), theme (optional hint string)}. Output: JSON {question, rationale, field, alternatives (list of 2 other questions)}. Present the question to the user for approval before starting the debate.

skills.name: Creative Lateral Argument
skills.description: Finds the most surprising non-obvious argument by drawing from an unexpected field of knowledge. Input: JSON {question, position, round (1|2), fields (optional list), opponent_arguments (round 2 only)}. Output: JSON {argument, field, insight, confidence, main_point, approach}.

skills.name: Suggest Debate Topic
skills.description: Generates a surprising, thought-provoking debate question drawn from an unexpected field. Call this when the user has no topic or says 'surprise me'. Input: JSON {fields (optional list of fields), theme (optional hint string)}. Output: JSON {question, rationale, field, alternatives (list of 2 other questions)}. Present the question to the user for approval before starting the debate.

agents.description:
Produces the strongest evidence-based argument for a given question and position. Input: JSON with fields: question (string), position (string), round (1|2), opponent_arguments (array of prior arguments for round 2). Output: structured argument with key evidence, confidence score, and main point.
Evidence-Based Argument: Constructs the strongest possible argument using empirical evidence, data, and documented facts. Input: JSON {question, position, round (1|2), opponent_arguments (round 2 only)}. Output: JSON {argument, key_evidence, confidence, main_point, approach}.

skills.name: Evidence-Based Argument
skills.description: Constructs the strongest possible argument using empirical evidence, data, and documented facts. Input: JSON {question, position, round (1|2), opponent_arguments (round 2 only)}. Output: JSON {argument, key_evidence, confidence, main_point, approach}.

agents.description:
Impartial debate judge. Scores multiple arguments on clarity, relevance, logic, evidence, and persuasiveness. Picks a winner, explains why, and synthesizes a final combined answer when requested. Input: JSON with fields: question (string), arguments (array of argument objects), round (1|2), final (bool). Output: scores per agent, winner, winner_reason, and synthesis (if final=true).
Debate Judge: Scores all debater arguments, picks the winner, explains the verdict, and synthesizes a final answer. Input: JSON {question, arguments (array), round (1|2), final (bool)}. Output: JSON {scores, winner, winner_reason, synthesis}.

skills.name: Debate Judge
skills.description: Scores all debater arguments, picks the winner, explains the verdict, and synthesizes a final answer. Input: JSON {question, arguments (array), round (1|2), final (bool)}. Output: JSON {scores, winner, winner_reason, synthesis}.

agents.description:
Produces the strongest logic-based argument using reasoning, first principles, and deduction. Input: JSON with fields: question (string), position (string), round (1|2), opponent_arguments (array for round 2). Output: structured argument with logical chain, confidence score, and main point.
Logic-Based Argument: Constructs the strongest possible argument using pure reasoning and logical deduction. Input: JSON {question, position, round (1|2), opponent_arguments (round 2 only)}. Output: JSON {argument, logical_chain, confidence, main_point, approach}.

skills.name: Logic-Based Argument
skills.description: Constructs the strongest possible argument using pure reasoning and logical deduction. Input: JSON {question, position, round (1|2), opponent_arguments (round 2 only)}. Output: JSON {argument, logical_chain, confidence, main_point, approach}.

agents.description:
CodeAgent assistant
get_repo_context: Return human-readable context about a repo: its CLAUDE.md (architecture, conventions, tech stack, team norms) and any steering docs in .kiro/steering/. Call this before deep-diving into a codebase to understand its domain, coding patterns, and known constraints.
query_graph: Answer a natural-language question about a codebase by traversing its knowledge graph. Returns relevant nodes and edges as structured text. Use mode='bfs' (default) for broad exploration or mode='dfs' for depth-first tracing. Increase depth (max 6) to follow more hops. context_filter narrows traversal to specific edge relation types (e.g. ['calls', 'imports']). IMPORTANT: nodes returned have source_file and source_location fields. To understand what a method or class actually does, always follow up with read_source using those fields — node labels alone are not enough to answer questions about logic or behaviour.
get_node: Look up a specific node in a repo's knowledge graph by class name, method name, file path, or any label. Returns the node's source location, type, community, degree, and immediate neighbors. To read the actual code body, follow up with read_source using the returned source_file and source_location.
list_repos: List all configured code repositories and their graph index status. Returns repo names, node/edge counts, last refresh time, and health.
refresh_status: Get the last refresh log and manifest metadata for a specific repository. Shows what files were processed, how long it took, and any errors.
force_refresh: Trigger an immediate re-index of a repository, bypassing the 60-second scheduler. Set full_rebuild=true to discard the existing graph and rebuild from scratch. Returns a summary of what changed.
reload_config: Hot-reload codeagent.yaml without restarting the server. Call this after a new repo has been added to the config file to make it immediately available for indexing and querying. Returns the updated list of active repos.
read_source: Read a code snippet from a source file at a known location. Call this after query_graph or get_node returns a source_file + source_location to read the actual method or class body — node labels alone are not enough to answer questions about what the code does. source_file is the repo-relative path (e.g. 'apm-adaptor-payment/src/main/java/.../Foo.java'). source_location is a line reference like 'L25' or 'L25-60'. context_lines controls how many lines above/below to include (default 40).
get_callers: Find all callers (or dependents) of a symbol in the knowledge graph. Traverses incoming edges up to `depth` hops. Use relation_filter to limit to a specific edge type (e.g. 'calls', 'imports').
get_dependencies: List what a symbol calls, imports, or depends on. Traverses outgoing edges up to `depth` hops. Use relation_filter to limit to a specific edge type (e.g. 'calls', 'imports').
shortest_path: Find the shortest call/import path between two symbols in the knowledge graph. Useful for understanding how one component reaches another.
get_impact: Blast-radius analysis: given a symbol, find everything that transitively depends on it. Answers 'if I change X, what might break?' by following incoming edges up to `depth` hops. Results are sorted by distance from the root symbol.

skills.name: get_repo_context
skills.description: Return human-readable context about a repo: its CLAUDE.md (architecture, conventions, tech stack, team norms) and any steering docs in .kiro/steering/. Call this before deep-diving into a codebase to understand its domain, coding patterns, and known constraints.

skills.name: query_graph
skills.description: Answer a natural-language question about a codebase by traversing its knowledge graph. Returns relevant nodes and edges as structured text. Use mode='bfs' (default) for broad exploration or mode='dfs' for depth-first tracing. Increase depth (max 6) to follow more hops. context_filter narrows traversal to specific edge relation types (e.g. ['calls', 'imports']). IMPORTANT: nodes returned have source_file and source_location fields. To understand what a method or class actually does, always follow up with read_source using those fields — node labels alone are not enough to answer questions about logic or behaviour.

skills.name: get_node
skills.description: Look up a specific node in a repo's knowledge graph by class name, method name, file path, or any label. Returns the node's source location, type, community, degree, and immediate neighbors. To read the actual code body, follow up with read_source using the returned source_file and source_location.

skills.name: list_repos
skills.description: List all configured code repositories and their graph index status. Returns repo names, node/edge counts, last refresh time, and health.

skills.name: refresh_status
skills.description: Get the last refresh log and manifest metadata for a specific repository. Shows what files were processed, how long it took, and any errors.

skills.name: force_refresh
skills.description: Trigger an immediate re-index of a repository, bypassing the 60-second scheduler. Set full_rebuild=true to discard the existing graph and rebuild from scratch. Returns a summary of what changed.

skills.name: reload_config
skills.description: Hot-reload codeagent.yaml without restarting the server. Call this after a new repo has been added to the config file to make it immediately available for indexing and querying. Returns the updated list of active repos.

skills.name: read_source
skills.description: Read a code snippet from a source file at a known location. Call this after query_graph or get_node returns a source_file + source_location to read the actual method or class body — node labels alone are not enough to answer questions about what the code does. source_file is the repo-relative path (e.g. 'apm-adaptor-payment/src/main/java/.../Foo.java'). source_location is a line reference like 'L25' or 'L25-60'. context_lines controls how many lines above/below to include (default 40).

skills.name: get_callers
skills.description: Find all callers (or dependents) of a symbol in the knowledge graph. Traverses incoming edges up to `depth` hops. Use relation_filter to limit to a specific edge type (e.g. 'calls', 'imports').

skills.name: get_dependencies
skills.description: List what a symbol calls, imports, or depends on. Traverses outgoing edges up to `depth` hops. Use relation_filter to limit to a specific edge type (e.g. 'calls', 'imports').

skills.name: shortest_path
skills.description: Find the shortest call/import path between two symbols in the knowledge graph. Useful for understanding how one component reaches another.

skills.name: get_impact
skills.description: Blast-radius analysis: given a symbol, find everything that transitively depends on it. Answers 'if I change X, what might break?' by following incoming edges up to `depth` hops. Results are sorted by distance from the root symbol.

agents.description: Renders technical content into polished documentation files. Given a structured explanation and a desired format (html, markdown, or slides), produces a complete ready-to-use file artifact with Mermaid diagrams and syntax highlighting.

skills.name: Render Html
skills.description: Renders technical analysis into a documentation.html file. Input: JSON with fields: format (html|markdown|slides), title (string), content (markdown string). Output: complete html file artifact.

skills.name: Render Markdown
skills.description: Renders technical analysis into a documentation.md file. Input: JSON with fields: format (html|markdown|slides), title (string), content (markdown string). Output: complete markdown file artifact.

skills.name: Render Slides
skills.description: Renders technical analysis into a slides.md file. Input: JSON with fields: format (html|markdown|slides), title (string), content (markdown string). Output: complete slides file artifact.

agents.description:
OMNI agentic gateway: pyhelper
compare_smoke_session: Compare all before/after CSV file pairs in a smoke test session. Returns PASSED if files are identical, FAILED with a diff summary if not. Rows are matched by content (not position) to avoid false positives when a row is inserted or deleted. Key columns for cell-level diffs are auto-detected from the CSV headers.
download_smoke_file_from_s3: Download a file from S3 into the smoke session folder (before/after). The S3 key is always built from the provided fields. FILE_TYPE is inferred in this priority order: (1) GL document type present → GL; (2) currency present (e.g. GBP, USD) → GROSS_INVOICE; (3) contract contains 'CP' → PARTNER_STATEMENT; (4) default → MERCHANT_STATEMENT. Always use the FULL contract identifier (e.g. '001-CM-000847', NOT just '000847'). partner_contract can be a contract number (e.g. '001-CP-000308') or the word 'Direct' for direct merchants. For GL files: pass gl_document_type (e.g. 'GL_EXTRACTS', 'GL_FX', 'GL_FX_VISA', 'GL_INTERBRANCH') and financial_institution (e.g. '0001', '0002', 'Malta') to build the key correctly. 
find_file_in_downloads: Search the server resources folder for a smoke test file matching the given type and optional requirements. Returns the most recently modified matching file, or 'found: false' if none exists. Only available in the 'before' phase — after deployment, use download_smoke_file_from_s3 instead.
save_existing_file_as_smoke: Copy a local file (found via find_file_in_downloads) into the smoke session folder under 'before'. Only available in the 'before' phase — after deployment, use download_smoke_file_from_s3 instead.
find_file_in_db: Query the metadata database for a file matching the given criteria. All filters are optional — omit any you don't need. If multiple rows match, one is chosen at random.
create_smoke_session: Create a new smoke test session. Returns session_id and the before/after folder paths. Call this once at the start of every smoke test run. Valid file_types: MERCHANT_STATEMENT, PARTNER_STATEMENT, MERCHANT_SKYTAB_INVOICE, MERCHANT_INVOICE, GL_EXTRACTS
get_session_status: Get the current state of a smoke test session, including which files have been saved.
get_latest_session: Return the most recently created smoke test session. Use this when the session_id is not known — for example when the user returns after deployment to collect the 'after' files and the session_id is no longer in context.
advance_session_to_after_phase: Mark that deployment is complete and advance the session to the 'after' phase. Call this when the user confirms the deployment is done.

skills.name: compare_smoke_session
skills.description: Compare all before/after CSV file pairs in a smoke test session. Returns PASSED if files are identical, FAILED with a diff summary if not. Rows are matched by content (not position) to avoid false positives when a row is inserted or deleted. Key columns for cell-level diffs are auto-detected from the CSV headers.

skills.name: download_smoke_file_from_s3
skills.description: Download a file from S3 into the smoke session folder (before/after). The S3 key is always built from the provided fields. FILE_TYPE is inferred in this priority order: (1) GL document type present → GL; (2) currency present (e.g. GBP, USD) → GROSS_INVOICE; (3) contract contains 'CP' → PARTNER_STATEMENT; (4) default → MERCHANT_STATEMENT. Always use the FULL contract identifier (e.g. '001-CM-000847', NOT just '000847'). partner_contract can be a contract number (e.g. '001-CP-000308') or the word 'Direct' for direct merchants. For GL files: pass gl_document_type (e.g. 'GL_EXTRACTS', 'GL_FX', 'GL_FX_VISA', 'GL_INTERBRANCH') and financial_institution (e.g. '0001', '0002', 'Malta') to build the key correctly.

skills.name: find_file_in_downloads
skills.description: Search the server resources folder for a smoke test file matching the given type and optional requirements. Returns the most recently modified matching file, or 'found: false' if none exists. Only available in the 'before' phase — after deployment, use download_smoke_file_from_s3 instead.

skills.name: save_existing_file_as_smoke
skills.description: Copy a local file (found via find_file_in_downloads) into the smoke session folder under 'before'. Only available in the 'before' phase — after deployment, use download_smoke_file_from_s3 instead.

skills.name: find_file_in_db
skills.description: Query the metadata database for a file matching the given criteria. All filters are optional — omit any you don't need. If multiple rows match, one is chosen at random.

skills.name: create_smoke_session
skills.description: Create a new smoke test session. Returns session_id and the before/after folder paths. Call this once at the start of every smoke test run. Valid file_types: MERCHANT_STATEMENT, PARTNER_STATEMENT, MERCHANT_SKYTAB_INVOICE, MERCHANT_INVOICE, GL_EXTRACTS

skills.name: get_session_status
skills.description: Get the current state of a smoke test session, including which files have been saved.

skills.name: get_latest_session
skills.description: Return the most recently created smoke test session. Use this when the session_id is not known — for example when the user returns after deployment to collect the 'after' files and the session_id is no longer in context.

skills.name: advance_session_to_after_phase
skills.description: Mark that deployment is complete and advance the session to the 'after' phase. Call this when the user confirms the deployment is done.

agents.description:
Analyzes a registered agent for security posture: HTTP surface probes (TLS, auth enforcement, reachability) and LLM analysis of the agent card and declared skills for over-broad scope, prompt-injection risk, and missing input guardrails. Returns a 0-100 score, risk level, summary, and actionable findings.
Scan Agent: Given a target agent's endpoint URL, agent card, and declared skills, run HTTP surface probes and an LLM card/skill risk analysis, returning a structured security assessment with score (0-100), risk level, summary, and findings.

skills.name: Scan Agent
skills.description: Given a target agent's endpoint URL, agent card, and declared skills, run HTTP surface probes and an LLM card/skill risk analysis, returning a structured security assessment with score (0-100), risk level, summary, and findings.

agents.description:
Takes a real-world address and building description, fetches a street-level photo via Google Street View, then uses FLUX.1 Kontext AI to render the described building into the real photo. Returns a photorealistic composite image.
visualize_location: Render a described building at a real-world location using street-level photography and AI image editing

skills.name: visualize_location
skills.description: Render a described building at a real-world location using street-level photography and AI image editing

agents.description:
Analyzes a the-M workflow canvas (orchestrators, agents, entry points, connections) and provides actionable advisory: missing configuration, routing logic gaps, orchestrator prompt quality, agent description clarity, structural issues, and security considerations. Supports multi-turn follow-up for prompt suggestions and detailed explanations.
Advise Workflow: Given a serialized workflow graph (nodes, edges, orchestrator system prompts, agent descriptions, entry point config), analyze it for completeness, routing logic, prompt quality, structural issues, and security posture. Returns a streamed advisory with issues, warnings, and actionable suggestions.

skills.name: Advise Workflow
skills.description: Given a serialized workflow graph (nodes, edges, orchestrator system prompts, agent descriptions, entry point config), analyze it for completeness, routing logic, prompt quality, structural issues, and security posture. Returns a streamed advisory with issues, warnings, and actionable suggestions.
